Longest runtime Best Picture winner?
What is mise-en-scène?
600.a: Arrangement of everything that appears in a shot — actors, lighting, sets, props, costumes

600.q: What is the Kuleshov Effect?
600.a: Audience emotion is shaped by the shot preceding an actor's neutral face — a key editing discovery

600.q: What does auteur theory mean?
600.a: A film director is the primary creative author, imprinting their personal vision across their work

600.q: What is German Expressionism in film?
600.a: A 1920s movement using distorted sets and dramatic lighting — e.g. Nosferatu, The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari

600.q: What film pioneered CGI for organic creatures?
What is a MacGuffin?
600.a: An object motivating characters but ultimately unimportant — a Hitchcock concept

600.q: What is the Bechdel Test?
600.a: Two women must talk to each other about something other than a man — a measure of female representation

600.q: Which film did Orson Welles direct at age 25?
What is French New Wave cinema?
600.a: 1950s–60s French film movement rejecting traditional conventions — directors include Godard and Truffaut

600.q: What is the 180-degree rule?
600.a: Two characters must always have the same left-right relationship — crossing it confuses the audience

600.q: What is Italian Neorealism?
600.a: Post-WWII film movement using non-professional actors and real locations — e.g. Bicycle Thieves

600.q: What is a tracking shot?
600.a: Camera physically moves through the scene following the subject

600.q: What is Dogme 95?
600.a: A filmmaking manifesto by Lars von Trier and Thomas Vinterberg — rejecting special effects, requiring location shooting

600.q: Who invented the close-up shot?
600.a: D.W. Griffith is often credited — he popularised it in early silent cinema

600.q: What is Soviet Montage theory?
600.a: The collision of images creates new meaning beyond each individual image — developed in 1920s Soviet cinema

600.q: What is the Spielberg face?
600.a: A reaction shot where a character looks at something off-screen with wonder, letting audience project emotion

600.q: What is neo-noir?
600.a: A genre updating classic noir conventions to modern settings — e.g. Blade Runner, Chinatown, L.A. Confidential

600.q: What is ludonarrative dissonance?
600.a: The conflict between a story and gameplay mechanics — popularized in film criticism too

600.q: What is a diegetic sound in film?
600.a: Sound that comes from within the film's world — characters can hear it (music from a radio, footsteps)

600.q: Who was Stanley Kubrick?
600.a: A perfectionist director known for 2001, Full Metal Jacket, The Shining — known for groundbreaking visual style
